# Transcrição: TBT-Week-13

- **Fonte:** TBT-Week-13
- **Duração:** 57:06
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

It makes me a little longer than I expected. OK, guys. So I want to welcome everyone to Total Breakthrough Training. This is Master Unit 4. We're going to be talking about Tony's work with Kristen and Frank Alioto, and also presenting some strategies that we've developed that will help you understand this intervention here. We're going to start off with a strategy and a dynamic. And that's pretty universal, even though that it's a very specific situation. And you'll see this used in Tony, where he works with Frank and Kristen in that short intervention. Even though Frank is a paraplegic and they have a very specific type of situation, the underlying relationship dynamic is universal. And we call this a reaction cycle. It's a pattern of interaction between two people and a very emotional disconnect between them and a division where they feel that they can't meet each other's needs. So it's really common when you're working with a couple that there's an emotion that one member gets, or a way that they express themselves, or something they say that throws the other member off their game into a pattern of helplessness. And this helplessness creates pain for both people. So there's three basic steps. In step one, the person A in the couple, they express themselves. And this can be a man or a woman in a way that's very emotional, very intense, and where they're upset. So they might complain, they might curse, they might show their frustration. Now, partner B reacts emotionally and takes it personally, basically. So people react in different ways to this. Partner B can react with compassion or patience or care, and they can roll up their sleeves and try to help their partner. And that would be a very direct way of meeting partner A's needs, and the reaction cycle would be cut short, and there wouldn't be a problem. We wouldn't be talking about it, right? Now, we're talking about situations where partner B reacts to their partner's emotion with helplessness, with anger, with annoyance, with disconnecting, with guilt, or with shame. And when people... And, Mark, if I could add to that, sometimes partner B reacts by escalating partner A's reaction. So let's say a wife gets upset and throws an insult at her husband. The husband might throw a greater insult at the wife, and that's how a fight begins. Yes, exactly. And what you need to get is that partner B, that there is a helplessness. I mean, all those responses are not people's preferred responses in general, you know, getting angry, escalating, going into shame, disconnecting, becoming muted. Those are ways that people respond to something that's very sore, like there's been a stacking of pain, and so instead of going to help, they go into pain.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

And so they might feel like their partner's complaints or their emotions are unreasonable, or it's not what they agreed upon, or it's too broad, or it's blaming, or it's something that no one can control. And very often it's a cultural difference, right? One person comes from a culture where you never complain or express emotions strongly, negatively, right? You're always positive, you're as quiet, you don't whinge, as the New Zealanders say. The other person comes from a culture where you express yourself strongly all the time, whether it's positive or negative, you call it out, you say, you know, someone asks you how you're doing, you say, ah, you know, I have some problems. And that's a cultural thing, right? And so sometimes there's a cultural disconnect between two people, and it can go deep. Sometimes partner B is still reacting to something that their partner told them days before. So partner A has an upset, and partner B is like, ah, again, and you know, they're responding to something that happened like a week ago, but they can't let go of it, and therefore they go into helplessness again. Also what brings people into helplessness is when the complaint is about something very general and broad and like historical, like this will never change, or this has always been this way. Partner B feels helpless, and when you feel helpless, you disconnect from the feeling that you can help the other person, right? And people go into comfort in themselves, meaning they go into a self-soothing mode. So when you, when someone goes into shame, guilt, helplessness, and it's something that the literature on learned helplessness doesn't talk about very much, is that that is secondary gain right there. So when someone feels, oh, I can't help it, oh, I can't, I just, you know, it's so hard, and they go into, and that's secondary gain, that's the person going into giving themselves significance, giving themselves connection, giving to themselves instead of being in the relationship. So in this case, when Kristen would be, you know, helping Frank, caring for Frank, she'd be moving him, and she'd forget to take his glasses off and accidentally hurt him, and then she'd yell at herself for being stupid, and Frank doesn't know how to handle her complaint. So he feels guilty and ashamed of being a burden to her, and then she feels like an even worse person. So in this situation, it's very clear, you know, and Frank is a paraplegic, and it's a very strong kind of fact between them, and he can't change that, he's obligated to, you know, Kristen takes care of him, he's obligated to her, and so he doesn't, for instance, escalate because of the situation. Now in other situations with partners and couples, the dynamic is it's not so clear. So a person may be complaining or expressing emotion about any number of topics that is bothering them, giving them pain, and person B will go into helplessness and they'll go into defensive, and they might feel blamed, and they might feel like they're going out of the agreement, or that they're going out of, you know, the policy of how you speak, and person B reacts with anger, with guilt, with shame, and emotional withdrawal,

## [06:04 - 09:05]

and all of these are responses to feeling helpless. So part three of this pattern is the person A now reacts emotionally to person B, so now it's stacked. In Kristen's case, when Frank responds by shutting down and feeling shame, she feels like a terrible person, and she had meant her frustration to be aimed at herself. She didn't intend it to be a direct criticism of him, and yet it was taken that way. And so this is the thing about when people complain and express themselves, we all like to feel like we have the right to feel bad to complain, you know, without affecting the people around us, but the truth is that when we do, we create work for the people around us who care for us, right? And so all of us do this, and it doesn't mean that you can't express yourself, it's important to do that, but, and for some people, it's really important, urgent to express yourself, they need to express themselves more, but just be aware that the trigger pattern here is that it does, you know, it does create a dialogue between the people, and so when I, or you or anyone else, goes into a complaint, that the other person may go into the defensive and may get helpless. So how do you respond to the triple trigger? We have to realize that when people complain or express intense emotion, it's also what we call an offer, and this is a strategy that Magalie and I developed, and an offer is an invitation for the other person to help you. So the objective here is to get the couple out of learned helplessness about each other's emotions and be able to reassure and respond to each other in a way that's satisfying, and that is a response to the offer. So we have a whole training on offer and block, but we'll give you here just the one-minute version. When partner A complains, that is an offer, that is a veiled invitation for partner B to get involved in a conversation. So almost anything can be taken as an offer. Like you can say my, you know, how I hurt myself, and it's an offer for your partner to say, oh, where does it hurt, you know? Or when you say it's a nice day outside, it's a, it can be an offer to talk about the weather. So now when someone makes an offer... Wait, wait, wait, wait, Mark. It also can be an invitation to action. It can be an offer to go for a walk. Absolutely. Yeah, so it's an invitation to engage. Now, when someone makes you an offer, you have the option to return the offer and say it's beautiful out, and glad it stopped raining, or you can block the offer. An example of blocking, or I don't like the weather, or I don't care about it, or you fail to respond completely and you shut down, and that's also a block. And so the takeaway here is that the couple needs to recognize that the complaint is an invitation. It's an offer, and for the partner to get involved with them emotionally. So when Frank shuts down and gets ashamed and feels like, you know, he's feeling for her, and he's feeling bad, and he's feeling like what a burden he is and stuff,

## [09:05 - 12:05]

and he's not speaking to her, and he's not responding to her, that's actually a block. So that's why she gets even sadder when he goes into that. So Tony took them through a pretend strategy to help retrain them so that when Kristen has those feelings, Frank can respond to her in a way that helps her. So Tony started by taking them through an induction, inviting them to notice things that they hadn't noticed before. So they looked around and they saw visually things in the landscape and things in the architecture that they hadn't noticed before. It primes them for resensitizing and opening up to new stimulus. Tyna Tony then asked Kristen to get into the state and recreate the state, pretend it, where she gets emotional and crazy, as he says, and then he asks her, what is it that triggers you and puts you into that state? So she explains that when she's taking care of Frank, she forgets something and she unintentionally hurts him and she yells at herself. So Tony encourages her to get into that state now to recreate it. And Mark, if I could add some of the exact words. I think it's important for us to all take note of exact ways that master coaches and hypnotists use language. So Tony says, see what you see, feel what you feel, then say it the way you say it, with the volume you say it. That's great. So that's just something that when you're watching and taking notes, I think it's really a good idea to take down exact wording. Yeah, that's great. And so, and he really had to, it's also great to listen to see how many ways that he reassured her, because she was not eager to go into that. So he reassured her that it was safe, that it was important, that he knows that there's a real feeling behind it, and got her into that state. So next Tony asked Frank to focus on her and be fully present and reassuring and loving to her, and they discover what really helps her to respond. So it's really important that they, and it's important, it was really fast in this case, and Tony had already really eyeballed this couple a lot at that point. But it's important for the coach to really pay attention and see what the responsiveness is like between the couple. So in this case, Kristen responded well to that expression to him. She responded well, especially to being touched, and Tony noticed that, and he encouraged that and magnified it and directed Frank to do more of that. And finally, Tony asked Kristen to talk about how she feels when Frank does this to reassure her, and so she confirmed that she feels better, and therefore, this is the, you know, the reaction cycle has been corrected in this way. And, you know, it's something that becomes the basis of this couple's relationship as they go through the rest of the challenges.

## [12:05 - 15:09]

Now in this case, this reaction cycle was fairly simple because it was like a presence exercise, and it was with, you know, someone who had limited means. I mean, Frank had only certain ways that he could give to her because he is physically, you know, incapacitated. But this can be used in all sorts of different, there's all sorts of different complaints and ways that you can respond to the person. So, you guys, would you like to discuss this? And we're open to questions as well. Press star two to raise your hand. And just to remind everybody, you can ask us questions about what you've been studying. You can ask us about your own life, about clients that you're working with, family members. Great. All right, so I have a caller from 315. 315? Oh, hi. Hey there. Hi. I had a question but it wasn't related to this. Is that okay? Okay. My question is, do you have any ideas or strategies for someone with aphobia of vomiting, a metaphobia? Aphobia of vomiting. Then it means, is this a man or a woman? A woman, she's 27. And she is afraid of vomiting or she's afraid of what? Yes, she's afraid of vomiting. She has to have a tonsillectomy and she's very afraid that she's going to feel sick and vomit. Did this fear exist before she knew she had to have a tonsillectomy? Yes, she said she struggled with this for years. And there was no memory of anything that started it for her. Okay. Has she vomited? What are her memories of vomiting? She... I asked her that. She said she never... She doesn't have any memories of it. She said that she has in the past had success using exposure therapy. And she has gotten to the point now where she can watch someone on TV vomiting and she can tolerate her dog vomiting, but she can't... She can't get as far as herself. Is she afraid of vomiting in public or in private also or where? Both, yes. And by herself or with other people? Either. Is she afraid of the pain or of the sensation or what?

## [15:09 - 18:10]

The sensation, yes. Just the idea of it and the sensation. You know, one thing that's very interesting about vomiting is that it's involuntary. We can't actually control when our body has to get rid of something or when there's a gag reflex. We're out of control of our body. It takes over. So I would be curious what other things happen in this person's life that may be out of their control? Or quite other ways that she's afraid of being out of control. You can also ask, can she burp? Sometimes, can she burp? I don't know. I haven't asked her. It's a related situation where you're letting your digestive stuff go buck up. But many people have this fear, but yet when they come down with food poisoning, their body still vomits. It's one of those things that it's a truth. They have the fear and it's a truth that their body will vomit anyways. What she does is a strategy, is she focuses on being as healthy as she could possibly be. She doesn't want to be around people who are sick. She makes sure she takes vitamins and antioxidants. She eats well. She really, really, really is healthy. She makes that sort of strategy to avoid as much as possible. Okay, so the fear serves her well. Right, yeah. And when did this start? Does she remember when she started having this fear? I mean, she's 27 and she remembers having it since she was young. And she also says that she... I'm looking at my nose here. She's a procrastinator. She has a lot of insecurities on communication with people. And she doesn't know if that's related or not. So we've been talking about that a little bit too. And what kind of relationships does she have? Does she have a boyfriend, does she have girlfriends? She's a recent college graduate and she's living at home with her parents. ## [18:10 - 21:13]

She doesn't... Okay. ...he has girlfriends. No boyfriend. Is it possible that the fear of vomiting is keeping her at home longer than she would otherwise be? Good. Something good to look into, yeah. Right, yeah, because it's that life stage of living home. Right, the secondary gain of having it. That's right. I would also focus on the metaphor of vomiting. I had a woman, professor of clinical psychology, when you would take an exam, the exams were oral, verbal exams were her, she would say things like, if I now vomited on you, what would that mean? To get the student to explain metaphor. So then, you know, one would say, well, it might mean that you don't really like me, or that you actually hate me, or that you're disgusted by me, or that I make you sick. Oh, very good. Excellent, she would say. Yeah, that's what it would mean. You know, one way to think about vomiting, there's an interesting explanation of vomiting that comes from, actually comes from evolutionary psychology. And the question is, why do people vomit when they're seasick? Like when you're on a boat, why do people get nauseous and vomit? Why should you vomit instead of like, say, get a headache, or get, you know, a cramp? Or, you know, why should that be your response? And the reason is it's actually developed from evolution is that your body, it happens when you're in a room and there's a disconnect between what your visual field is and what you sense in your body. Meaning that when you feel like you're rocking back and forth, even though the room that you're in inside the boat feels like still and like you're on Earth. Like you're sitting on the ground. That's why the cure is to stand on the deck and move with the boat as if you were horseback riding. And so the question is, the reason, why would you vomit in response to that? And the best response that people have right now is that your body has the intelligence to think, to respond, to say, if I'm feeling different than what I'm seeing, I must have been poisoned. I must have eaten something bad and I need to regurgitate it. So it's way beyond our conscious scope of mind to think that we can control it or that we know why it's there. It's your body is responding and thinking that you have eaten something badly. And over millions of years, you know, it's been a successful strategy for, you know, bodies and genes to evolve. So it's a fear of having them made a mistake.
